comments:
Summary:
The paper studies the structural tractability of enumerating (possibly projected) solutions of CSP instances under the general tree-projection framework. It introduces the notion of tp-covered (both output-aware and output-independent variants), gives polynomial-delay enumeration algorithms parametric in a chosen decomposition method DM (ComputeAllSolutionsDM and ComputeCertifiedSolutionsDM), proves their correctness and WPD complexity when tp-covered conditions hold, and proves tightness/dichotomy results: necessity of the structural conditions (for tw and hw), evidence of hardness under standard complexity assumptions (FPT ≠ W[1]) for classes of bounded arity, and NP-hardness of producing certified solutions under certain circumstances. The work generalizes and unifies prior results (treewidth, hypertree-width, earlier local-consistency characterizations) to the tree-projection setting and addresses both promise and no-promise variants.

Strengths:
- The paper addresses an important and understudied computational task (enumeration with polynomial delay) in the CSP literature.
- The approach is very general: tree projections capture all standard decomposition methods; results therefore apply widely.
- The definitions (tp-covered, domain-restricted variables) are natural and the connection between GAC and tree projections is exploited effectively.
- The paper gives both algorithmic solutions and matching negative results (tightness/dichotomies) under standard complexity assumptions, strengthening the contributions.
- Theorems are clearly stated and the paper positions its contributions well with respect to prior work.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- Some proofs are only sketched in the provided text; full detailed proofs (or pointers to appendices) would help a reader assess all technical subtleties.
- The practical applicability is not discussed much: checking whether (A,O) is tp-covered involves tree-projection existence, and the tree-projection decision problem is NP-complete in general — the paper should discuss the practical consequences of this (though some of these concerns are addressed by the no-promise algorithm and by considering FPT variants, an explicit discussion would help).
- Complexity bounds are often given as “polynomial”; more precise dependence on input size and on parameters (e.g., widths) would be useful for implementers.
- The provided text contains some duplicated paragraphs and minor formatting/noise near the end; the final version should be cleaned.
- Examples and possibly experimental validation (even small-scale) would strengthen the paper’s practical impact and illuminate behavior of the algorithms.

Suggestions:
- Include full proofs or clearly direct the reader to a technical appendix with full proofs; this helps verify correctness of the more intricate claims.
- Discuss the complexity of verifying tp-covered in practice and how the algorithms behave when the property is not guaranteed (e.g., run-time costs of the DM view construction, and the handling of the ‘‘DM failure’’ case in the no-promise algorithm).
- Add worked examples tracing the algorithms on small instances (including both success and DM-failure cases) to make the technical concepts more accessible.
- If feasible, include an empirical section or at least a complexity-table comparing the costs of enforcing GAC on the views for common DMs (tw, hw, fractional hw) and how that affects delay in practice.

Conclusion:
This is a substantial, original, and technically strong contribution that advances understanding of enumeration complexity in CSPs in a general and unifying framework. After minor clarifications and cleanup, the paper will be a valuable reference for the field.
